name: Sovereignty-Resilience Framing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reframing the platform-dependency issue from privacy/ideology to concrete supplier-concentration and continuity-of-service risk unlocks political support within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and Folketinget's digital policy committee. Systemic: Shifting focus from anti-Big Tech rhetoric to institutional resilience language increases likelihood of formal procurement engagement by 60–80% compared to privacy-only messaging, because it aligns with Denmark's 2026–2029 digital strategy and existing public-sector risk frameworks. Strategic: This framing trade-off sacrifices immediate consumer awareness but establishes durable political cover for phase-two demonstrator funding and phase-three procurement language, making platform-neutrality a defensible public-sector decision rather than an ideological position.
options: Frame the intervention exclusively around supplier concentration, business continuity, and procurement neutrality in all policy documents and stakeholder meetings, explicitly avoiding anti-monopoly or anti-US messaging.

Develop a dual-track narrative: technical resilience framing for government procurement and policy documents, combined with separate civil-society and academic communications emphasizing privacy, sovereignty, and democratic accountability.

Establish a formal risk-assessment partnership with the Danish Central Business Register (Erhvervsstyrelsen) and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to publish an official 'Digital Identity Supplier Concentration Risk Report' that elevates the framing to institutional authority.
review: Controls political feasibility vs. institutional adoption resistance. Weakness: The options fail to consider how incumbent operators (MitID owner, banks) may actively counter-frame the narrative as alarmist or technically unfounded, requiring proactive media and stakeholder preemption.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Non-Production Boundary Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Committing to non-production, non-certified demonstrators (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, browser-based fallback) avoids impossible certification timelines and sidesteps MitID operator opposition to live-system integration. Systemic: A lean, defensible demonstrator scope reduces phase-two budget creep by 30–40% and preserves technical credibility by preventing false claims of production readiness, but creates risk that demonstrators will be dismissed as 'not real' by skeptical procurement decision-makers. Strategic: This trade-off exchanges immediate deployability for institutional legitimacy—the demonstrator becomes evidence of feasibility, not a product, which is essential for influencing procurement language in AltID without triggering incumbent legal or regulatory challenges.
options: Implement a strictly confined technical demonstrator against a simulated Danish public-sector OpenID Connect provider, with explicit versioning and disclaimers stating non-production status, and establish a formal governance board with external security reviewers to prevent scope creep.

Develop two complementary demonstrators in parallel—one mobile-oriented (FIDO2 on GrapheneOS) and one browser-based fallback (hardware token or web-standard authentication)—presented as separate evidence tracks to address both continuity-planning and platform-diversity concerns.

Pursue a licensing and reference-implementation model where the project publishes open-source authentication libraries and compliance checklists under an EU-friendly license, enabling third-party integration while maintaining the project's distance from production deployment and liability.
review: Controls scope ambition vs. credibility preservation. Weakness: The options fail to consider that a demonstrator's perceived weakness ('not production-ready') may itself become the primary objection from procurement stakeholders who want immediate solutions, not future-oriented evidence.

name: AltID Intervention Timing and Gating Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Aligning phase-three intervention with AltID's formal design and procurement gates (expected months 18–24) ensures the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-access recommendations can influence procurement language before architectural decisions solidify. Systemic: Early intervention (month 16 decision gate) increases the likelihood of inclusion in official AltID documentation by 40–60% compared to post-hoc input, but requires phase-one coalition-building and phase-two demonstrator credibility to be timing-critical dependencies, creating schedule risk if either slips more than 4–6 weeks. Strategic: This lever trades delivery-schedule pressure for strategic relevance—the project must hit AltID's decision windows or risk becoming a post-hoc document without procurement influence, forcing a pivot to EU standards and publication work as fallback positioning.
options: Establish a formal monthly steering committee with representatives from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the AltID program office, and the MitID operator from month 4 onward, creating a scheduled feedback loop that forces architectural trade-offs to be documented and enables mid-course intervention if AltID timelines shift.

Coordinate phase-two demonstrator delivery (month 16) to coincide with AltID's planned 'Substantial' assurance-level requirements review, positioning the demonstrator as direct evidence for platform-neutral and fallback-capable design options within AltID's formal architecture decision gates.

Implement a contingency-activation protocol: if AltID engagement is weak by month 16, immediately de-scope phase three toward publication, EU standards input, and coalition consolidation, with pre-authorized budget reallocation to extend phase-two demonstrator work and external security review rather than pursuing procurement influence with diminishing returns.
review: Controls strategic timing vs. institutional alignment pressure. Weakness: The options fail to consider that AltID's own timeline and budget pressures may cause procurement gates to shift unexpectedly, or that political turnover (Denmark's next election cycle) could deprioritize digital identity work entirely, invalidating timing assumptions.

name: Institutional Anchoring and Funding Continuity Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Locating the project at a Danish university or security-qualified research institution establishes institutional credibility, provides employment and workspace continuity, and signals non-partisan intent to both government and civil-society stakeholders. Systemic: Institutional anchoring reduces project-dependency risk by 50% compared to a temporary consortium, but introduces 12–18 week overhead for institutional governance approvals and may create perceived conflicts of interest if the host institution receives significant government research funding, requiring transparent disclosure and governance-layer independence. Strategic: This lever trades administrative overhead for political durability across electoral cycles and budget uncertainties—an institutionally embedded project survives political transitions and funding reshuffles better than a time-limited initiative, enabling long-term standards influence if AltID timelines slip or if phase-three procurement traction emerges slowly.
options: Establish the project as a dedicated research center or initiative within an existing Danish university (e.g., IT University of Copenhagen, Aarhus University, or a dedicated cybersecurity/digital-policy institute) with a multi-year hosting agreement, formal governance independence, and transparent funding disclosure.

Structure the project as a joint venture between a lead research institution and an independent civil-society organization (e.g., a Danish digital-rights nonprofit or privacy advocacy group), creating institutional credibility alongside advocacy independence and reducing perception of academic or governmental bias.

Secure a formal memorandum of understanding with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen stipulating that the project operates under a defined research mandate independent of internal procurement influence, with public reporting requirements and external advisory oversight, positioning the project as institutional but administratively and operationally independent.
review: Controls institutional legitimacy vs. independence perception. Weakness: The options fail to consider that excessive institutional embedding may reduce the project's ability to publish critical findings or engage in advocacy that contradicts host-institution or government policy, or that civil-society partnership may expose the project to accusations of activist bias.

name: EU Standards Amplification and Domestic Procurement Leverage Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Engaging with European Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS2 technical working groups amplifies the project's platform-neutrality and fallback-access messaging at the EU regulatory level, where procurement language can influence national implementation frameworks. Systemic: EU-level advocacy creates 20–35% additional regulatory pressure on Danish procurement decisions because EU framework requirements cascade to national implementations, but introduces a 4–6 month engagement lag (EU working-group cycles) and risk that EU requirements remain non-binding or that Denmark interprets them narrowly to preserve incumbent advantages. Strategic: This lever trades domestic autonomy for European regulatory leverage—the project positions itself as contributing to EU-wide resilience standards rather than pursuing purely national interest, making it harder for incumbent operators to dismiss as parochial, but also creating dependence on EU standards timelines and the risk that EU engagement without domestic procurement traction appears performative.
options: Commit dedicated project resources (policy coordinator role) to participate in all relevant European Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS2 compliance working groups, with the explicit goal of contributing platform-neutrality and certified-fallback language to formal EU technical guidance, EU procurement templates, and EU architecture recommendations that cascade to national implementations.

Develop a multi-country advocacy coalition spanning digital-sovereignty initiatives in Austria, Netherlands, and Germany, publishing a joint 'European Platform-Neutral Digital Identity Standards Brief' that positions platform-neutrality and fallback access as EU-wide resilience and interoperability concerns, amplifying domestic Danish procurement arguments through peer-country regulatory alignment.

Maintain EU engagement as a secondary work stream (5–10% of phase-three effort) focused on publishing research and standards input, while prioritizing domestic AltID procurement intervention and stakeholder coalition; use EU engagement only to support and amplify successful domestic outcomes rather than betting on EU-driven change.
review: Controls EU regulatory leverage vs. domestic procurement focus. Weakness: The options fail to consider that EU-level engagement may be perceived by Danish institutions as undermining national sovereignty or procurement autonomy, or that the project's EU input may have minimal impact if similar concerns are not already embedded in EU member-state negotiating positions.

name: Coalition Architecture and Competing-Interest Management
consequences: Immediate: Establishing formal coalition governance with written agreements on decision-making, public messaging, and dispute resolution prevents civil-society, academic, and government stakeholders from advancing conflicting narratives or demanding incompatible deliverables. Systemic: A well-structured coalition increases stakeholder retention by 65–75% and reduces internal conflict delays by 40–50%, but requires 8–12 weeks of governance design and formal documentation, creating phase-one schedule risk and potential perception among some civil-society partners that the structure is overly bureaucratic or constrains autonomous advocacy. Strategic: This lever trades agility and informal partnership flexibility for coalition durability—formalizing roles and decision pathways prevents coalition collapse due to mission drift or stakeholder opportunism, but may exclude more radical or strictly anti-corporate advocates who view formal governance as compromising integrity.
options: Establish a tiered coalition structure with three separate working groups (technical, policy, and civil-society/advocacy), each with distinct deliverable ownership, quarterly sync meetings at the lead institution, and a steering committee that resolves inter-group conflicts using a documented priority matrix weighted toward procurement intervention over advocacy positioning.

Create a formal coalition charter signed by all partner organizations (including government liaisons) that specifies decision-making thresholds (e.g., 75% consensus required for major public statements, unanimous agreement required for regulatory submissions), with a neutral external facilitator hired to mediate disputes and ensure that coalition decisions reflect the shared objective of procurement language inclusion.

Implement a rotating coalition leadership model where civil-society, academic, and government representatives jointly chair quarterly full-coalition meetings, with explicit responsibility matrices that prevent any single stakeholder from dominating public communications, and a pre-agreed escalation protocol for conflicts between government engagement and advocacy goals.
review: Controls coalition unity vs. stakeholder autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider that formal governance structures may be perceived as co-optation by more activist-oriented civil-society partners, or that government liaisons may view formal agreements as creating transparency and legal liabilities they wish to avoid.

name: Cross-Domain Technical Talent Coordination and Knowledge Continuity
consequences: Immediate: Designing a technical-team structure that avoids single-point-of-failure dependencies in mobile security, OpenID Connect, hardware tokens, or GrapheneOS deployment ensures that no key person's departure derails demonstrator delivery or technical credibility. Systemic: Implementing paired-specialist roles and structured knowledge-transfer protocols (e.g., fortnightly technical-documentation updates, peer-review requirements, and cross-training checkpoints) increases technical-continuity confidence by 50–60% and reduces phase-two rework risk by 30–35%, but adds 15–20% to technical-staffing costs and requires discipline to avoid becoming process overhead that slows iteration. Strategic: This lever trades specialized expertise depth for team resilience—distributed knowledge and formal handoff procedures ensure the project survives staff turnover (a realistic risk over 24 months), but may reduce the agility of small, tightly-coordinated technical teams and create perception that the project is over-managed or lacks technical heroism.
options: Hire two independent security consultants (one for mobile authentication/FIDO2, one for browser-based and hardware-token workflows) as half-time advisors from month 4 onward, with explicit responsibility to review and validate all technical decisions, conduct monthly knowledge-transfer sessions with the core technical team, and co-author technical sections of the feasibility report and demonstrator documentation.

Establish a formal knowledge-management system (e.g., a shared technical wiki hosted on the project's secure infrastructure) where all architectural decisions, API integrations, security assumptions, and protocol implementations are documented with rationale and alternatives, with mandatory peer review before code commits and quarterly external security audits that explicitly assess knowledge distribution and identify single-points-of-failure.

Create a 'technical redundancy rotation' where the lead technical person spends 20% of their time each quarter training a junior engineer or contractor on critical demonstrator components, with deliverables including written implementation guides, video walkthroughs of complex subsystems, and a formal competency assessment confirming the junior person can independently manage the domain if needed.
review: Controls technical specialization vs. team fragility. Weakness: The options fail to consider that security-sensitive identity research may require clearance or institutional vetting that makes rapid hiring or contractor engagement difficult, or that external advisors may have competing commitments that limit their responsiveness during critical phases.

name: Regulatory Pathway and Certification-Barrier Navigation
consequences: Immediate: Mapping the regulatory and certification requirements for fallback-authentication paths under eIDAS2, NSIS, and Danish data-protection law (GDPR + DPA) in phase one identifies blockers, required approvals, and timelines before phase-two demonstrator work is locked into an infeasible architecture. Systemic: Early regulatory engagement with Datatilsynet (Danish Data Protection Authority) and relevant EU digital-identity regulators reduces phase-two rework risk by 45–55% and accelerates phase-three procurement language by establishing that fallback paths can meet 'Substantial' assurance without fundamental law changes, but introduces 6–10 week engagement delays and risk that regulators issue guidance that contradicts the project's architectural assumptions, forcing costly pivot. Strategic: This lever trades schedule agility for regulatory de-risking—proactive dialogue with authorities prevents late-stage certification rejection, but may expose the project to informal pressure to narrow demonstrator scope or to avoid messaging that regulators perceive as critical of current MitID certification practices.
options: Commission an independent regulatory-pathway assessment from a law firm specializing in EU digital identity and data protection, with explicit deliverables identifying all certification, approval, and disclosure requirements; timing and decision gates; and regulatory contacts for phase-three procurement engagement; published as a confidential project working document and formal regulatory-engagement roadmap.

Establish a formal advisory panel with representatives from Datatilsynet, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's legal team, and a Danish bar-association digital-law specialist, meeting quarterly to review demonstrator architecture, proposed fallback-path designs, and procurement language for regulatory feasibility and to issue non-binding advisory opinions that the project can reference during phase-three stakeholder engagement.

Pursue a formal pre-approval or guidance-request letter with Datatilsynet and the relevant EU digital-identity authorities (via Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) during phase one, asking for written confirmation that a platform-neutral fallback authentication path can comply with NSIS 'Substantial' requirements without amendment to existing law, positioning any regulatory blockage as explicit authority rejection rather than project risk.
review: Controls regulatory clarity vs. institutional friction. Weakness: The options fail to consider that formal regulatory requests may signal the project's intent to authorities before civil-society and procurement coalitions are ready, or that regulators may view the project as attempting to circumvent incumbent certification by seeking alternative paths.

name: Credibility Amplification and Evidence-Based Procurement Authority
consequences: Immediate: Publishing the phase-one feasibility report through a peer-reviewed venue (academic conference, government research program, or independent research authority) rather than solely as a project document establishes third-party validation and external authority that incumbent operators cannot easily dismiss as advocacy. Systemic: Third-party peer review and publication increase procurement decision-makers' confidence in the demonstrator viability by 40–55% and create durable evidence artifacts that survive political transitions, but introduces 4–8 week publication delays, requires accepting reviewer feedback that may weaken claims, and creates risk that published findings are cited or misquoted in ways the project cannot control. Strategic: This lever trades message control for institutional credibility—the project must release findings to external scrutiny and accept editorial constraints, but gains the ability to reference independent validation when confronting procurement skeptics, making it significantly harder for operators to dismiss the work as biased.
options: Submit the feasibility report for peer review through a recognized Nordic or EU research program (e.g., the EU's Digital Europe Programme research track, a Danish research council digital-sovereignty initiative, or a recognized cybersecurity research conference), with explicit commitment to incorporate reviewer feedback and to publish within 8–10 weeks of phase-one completion, accepting delay and editorial constraints as the cost of credibility.

Engage an independent external advisory board (comprising 4–6 recognized security researchers, EU digital-identity architects, and procurement experts) to conduct a formal technical and strategic review of all phase-two demonstrators and phase-three procurement language, with published review summaries that the project can cite as evidence of independent validation during stakeholder engagement.

Establish a formal partnership with the Danish Technological Institute (DTI) or equivalent quasi-governmental research authority to jointly sponsor and oversee the demonstrator work and publish findings under joint authorship, leveraging DTI's institutional credibility and government relationships while maintaining project editorial independence through a co-authorship governance structure.
review: Controls narrative control vs. external validation. Weakness: The options fail to consider that peer review and external advisory boards may produce inconvenient findings (e.g., that fallback paths create unacceptable assurance gaps) that undermine the project's advocacy, or that sharing early drafts with external reviewers may leak findings to incumbent operators before the project is ready for confrontation.

name: Implementation Assurance and Procurement-Language Durability
consequences: Immediate: Drafting explicit procurement-language specifications in phase two (not phase three) and stress-testing them against likely incumbent counter-arguments and technical objections creates durable, defensible language that cannot be easily reinterpreted or diluted during AltID implementation. Systemic: Pre-authoring and formally reviewing procurement language with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen legal teams and AltID architects by month 14 increases the likelihood that phase-three recommendations are adopted verbatim or near-verbatim by 50–65%, but requires early commitment to specific wording that may appear overly prescriptive if demonstrator results reveal new technical constraints, creating risk of language that is technically unsound or that operators successfully challenge as unachievable. Strategic: This lever trades technical flexibility for implementation certainty—the project must crystallize procurement language before phase-three concludes, but gains assurance that any approved language will be enforced during implementation rather than reinterpreted as advisory or deferred pending 'further study.'
options: Develop a formal 'Procurement Language & Implementation Specification' document in phase two that proposes exact wording for AltID contracts, vendor RFPs, and compliance requirements, with explicit technical rationale for each clause and pre-planned responses to likely operator objections (e.g., 'This requirement is technically achievable on iOS via [specific pathway]'), reviewed and approved by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and external legal counsel before month 16.

Create a 'Conformance Testing and Validation Framework' that specifies how fallback-path and platform-neutrality claims will be verified during AltID implementation, with concrete test cases, certification checklists, and third-party audit procedures, positioning the project as the arbiter of technical compliance and preventing operators from self-certifying compliance without independent oversight.

Negotiate a formal 'Implementation Assurance Clause' with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, documented as a signed commitment, that any procurement language derived from the project's recommendations will include phase-gate checkpoints requiring external technical review (potentially by the project team or successor body) before advancing to live deployment, ensuring that operator workarounds or implementation drift is detected and corrected before scale.
review: Controls implementation fidelity vs. adaptive implementation risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID operators may view formal implementation-assurance clauses as an unacceptable constraint on their operational flexibility, or that locking procurement language before full demonstrator results are available may force the project to advocate for technically unsound requirements that are later challenged or waived.

name: Incumbent Operator Antagonism and Preemptive De-Escalation
consequences: Immediate: Engaging formally with the MitID operator and major banking stakeholders in phase one—before the project's public positioning is locked and before demonstrators are built—creates opportunity to frame the project as complementary resilience enhancement rather than as existential threat to their business model. Systemic: Structured early engagement with incumbent operators increases probability of their neutral (rather than active-hostile) posture by 45–60% and reduces risk of legal or regulatory retaliation, but reveals the project's technical direction prematurely, invites operator input that may pressure scope reduction, and risks that operators use early engagement to lobby Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to preempt or defund the project before phase two. Strategic: This lever trades operational secrecy and design autonomy for reduced antagonism—the project must share enough architectural detail early to demonstrate non-threatening intent, but runs risk of operators extracting commitments or vetoes that constrain later options, making the negotiation itself a high-stakes decision point.
options: Schedule formal bilateral meetings with the MitID operator (DSB) and representatives from the Danish Bankers Association in month 2–3, explicitly framing the project as resilience and contingency planning (not replacement), presenting a confidentiality agreement that permits technical discussion without public commitment, and documenting operator concerns and stated blockers in a formal meeting record that becomes phase-one deliverable and shapes demonstrator scope accordingly.

Establish a formal 'Incumbent Operator Advisory Panel' with DSB and banking representatives meeting quarterly alongside the broader coalition, with guaranteed seats on the technical advisory board, explicit veto power over any demonstrator claims that contradict their system design, and contractual acknowledgment that the project will not publicly criticize existing MitID architecture—trading independence for institutional acceptance.

Develop a 'Fallback Path Operating Model' document in phase one that explicitly positions fallback authentication as insurance against platform policy changes, geopolitical risk, or edge-case outages (not replacement), and present it to the MitID operator as a joint public-relations benefit (demonstrating Danish resilience) rather than as criticism, inviting DSB to co-author or formally endorse the fallback concept before phase two.
review: Controls adversarial risk vs. operational independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider that early engagement may be dismissed by incumbent operators as performative or that operators may use operator-advisory-panel seats to systematically block or water down phase-two demonstrator scope, extracting veto power that undermines the project's technical agenda.

name: Diversified Financing Architecture and Contingency Funding Paths
consequences: Immediate: Securing funding from multiple sources (government, EU research programs, private digital-sovereignty foundations, and technology-neutral industry sponsors) rather than relying on a single government appropriation reduces political budget-cut exposure and extends project continuity if one funding stream is interrupted. Systemic: Multi-source financing increases project-survival probability by 50–65% if government funding encounters mid-project delays or political reprioritization, but introduces 8–12 weeks of additional fundraising work in phase one and creates administrative overhead managing multiple donor reporting requirements and governance constraints, potentially reaching 10–15% of total budget. Strategic: This lever trades administrative simplicity and exclusive government-partnership clarity for financial resilience—the project must diversify income streams and manage multiple funder relationships, but gains insurance against political budget cycles and can continue essential work if government funding pauses.
options: Launch a parallel fundraising campaign in parallel with government-budget negotiations (months 1–4), targeting EU Digital Europe Programme research funding, Nordic technology-independence initiatives, and private digital-sovereignty foundations (e.g., Mozilla Foundation, NDT Foundation), with explicit coordination to ensure that non-government funding covers phase-one policy and coalition work while government funding secures technical demonstrator costs.

Establish a 'Project Sustainability Endowment' fundraising target of 2.0–2.5 million DKK from private donors, technology-neutral industry sponsors, and EU sources during phase one, held in a dedicated account and allocated only if government funding encounters 3+ month delays or mid-project cuts, with transparent governance that prevents political pressure to release endowment funds unnecessarily.

Structure the project with explicit phase-gate funding architecture where each phase (1, 2, 3) can be independently funded and completed, allowing a phase-two-only scenario if government funding is withdrawn after phase one, and prioritizing phase-three procurement work as the highest-ROI outcome (requiring only 2–3 weeks of phase-one deliverables to proceed independently).
review: Controls financial autonomy vs. donor relationship management. Weakness: The options fail to consider that accepting funding from private donors or EU sources may create perception of bias or external influence (especially if EU funding appears to override Danish autonomy), or that multiple funders with conflicting reporting timelines and governance requirements may fragment the project's decision-making and slow execution.

name: Stakeholder Cognition and Lived-Experience Demonstrator Engagement
consequences: Immediate: Organizing structured 'demonstrator experience' sessions where Digitaliseringsstyrelsen officials, AltID architects, procurement decision-makers, and relevant civil servants actually authenticate using the phase-two fallback-path demonstrators (rather than reading technical specifications) creates embodied understanding of feasibility and user-experience that abstract documentation cannot replicate. Systemic: Hands-on stakeholder engagement with working demonstrators increases decision-maker confidence in fallback-path viability by 50–70% and shifts internal AltID conversations from 'Is this possible?' to 'How do we integrate this?', but requires demonstrator stability and polish sufficient for non-technical audiences, consuming 15–25% of phase-two engineering budget and creating schedule risk if demonstrators encounter critical bugs during stakeholder sessions. Strategic: This lever trades demonstrator completeness for strategic impact—the project must invest in user-experience polish and end-to-end stability for stakeholder engagement, not just technical functionality, ensuring that fallback-path viability is convincingly demonstrated rather than theoretically argued.
options: Organize a series of 'AltID Stakeholder Demonstrator Sessions' in months 15–16, inviting Digitaliseringsstyrelsen digital-identity leadership, AltID program architects, and procurement officials to hands-on authentications using FIDO2/WebAuthn and browser-based fallback demonstrations, facilitated by the project team with explicit focus on user experience, authentication speed, and integration friction points, with session recordings and usability feedback collected for phase-three procurement language.

Develop a 'Portable Demonstrator Kit' (a physical or virtual environment) that can be deployed on stakeholder computers or in workshop settings, allowing officials to authenticate against a simulated Danish public-sector OpenID Connect provider without requiring specialized hardware or developer setup, reducing friction and enabling wider participation from non-technical decision-makers.

Commission an independent usability study (conducted by an external HCI or UX research firm, months 14–16) of both demonstrators focused on decision-maker and end-user perception of fallback-path friction, security, and trustworthiness, with published findings that the project can reference during phase-three stakeholder engagement as evidence that fallback paths are not unacceptably degraded compared to platform-mediated authentication.
review: Controls engineering ROI vs. stakeholder influence. Weakness: The options fail to consider that polishing demonstrators for stakeholder experience may divert engineering resources from technical robustness, or that stakeholder experience sessions may raise expectations of production readiness that undermine the project's non-production framing when operators later demand feature parity or support timelines.

name: AltID Procurement-Path Diversification and Inertia Bypass
consequences: Immediate: Identifying alternative procurement intervention points beyond the primary AltID program (e.g., regional public-administration identity pilots, EU-funded digital-identity testbeds, or bank consortium alternatives to MitID) creates fallback routes to influence if the primary AltID procurement path encounters political or operational blockage. Systemic: Diversifying intervention targets increases probability of at least one procurement success by 40–55% (reducing dependence on AltID alone), but divides phase-three engagement effort across multiple initiatives with different decision timelines and stakeholders, consuming 20–30% more coordination overhead and risking that the project's message becomes diluted or inconsistent across concurrent procurement streams. Strategic: This lever trades focused AltID influence for broader procurement penetration—the project must prepare and maintain engagement with multiple procurement pathways simultaneously, but dramatically increases the likelihood that some platform-neutral or fallback-capable language makes it into operational procurement, even if primary AltID channel is blocked.
options: Map all active and planned public-sector and quasi-governmental digital-identity initiatives in Denmark (regional eHealth portals, social-benefit authentication systems, public-employee identity platforms) in phase one, identify 2–3 secondary procurement windows with realistic phase-three timelines, and develop separate but aligned procurement-language variants for each pathway, positioned as complementary to AltID rather than competitive.

Establish formal engagement with the European Digital Identity Wallet testbed pilots (if Denmark participates) and coordinate to ensure that any Danish pilot procurements include fallback-path and platform-neutrality evaluation criteria, creating a parallel validation pathway that, if successful, demonstrates viability at the EU level and creates de facto pressure on AltID to adopt similar approaches.

Develop a 'Platform-Neutral eIDAS2 Compliance Toolkit' (a reusable procurement template, vendor-assessment checklist, and architecture decision guide) in phase two that can be offered to any public-sector organization procuring digital-identity infrastructure, positioning the project as a public resource and multiplying intervention leverage across the entire public-sector identity ecosystem, not just AltID.
review: Controls procurement focus vs. message fragmentation. Weakness: The options fail to consider that pursuing multiple procurement pathways simultaneously may appear opportunistic or unfocused to stakeholders, or that secondary procurement initiatives may explicitly defer to AltID's decisions, rendering parallel engagement efforts redundant and wasteful.

name: Technology Horizon Scanning and Adaptive Scope Calibration
consequences: Immediate: Establishing a formal technology and regulatory horizon-scanning process in phase one (monitoring emerging EU eIDAS2 guidance, iOS/Android security API evolution, quantum-resistant authentication research, and device-integrity standards development) ensures that demonstrator scope and procurement language remain technically and legally current across the 24-month project timeline. Systemic: Quarterly horizon-scanning reviews with appropriate pivots to demonstrator or procurement language increase the project's adaptive capacity by 50–60% and reduce risk of phase-three recommendations becoming technically obsolete, but introduces 4–6 weeks of additional phase-one analysis and requires discipline to distinguish between genuine technical shifts (requiring scope adjustment) and false-alarm industry hype, risking either over-investment in tracking or missed critical changes. Strategic: This lever trades locked design certainty for adaptive relevance—the project must remain flexible about technical direction and procurement language if underlying conditions shift, but ensures that final deliverables reflect current technical reality and regulatory guidance rather than 24-month-old assumptions.
options: Establish a 'Technology Horizon and Risk Steering Committee' comprising the project lead, an external mobile-security researcher, an EU digital-identity standards representative, and a Danish procurement official, meeting quarterly to review emerging iOS/Android security APIs, eIDAS2 regulatory guidance, quantum-resistant authentication standards, and device-integrity frameworks, with authority to recommend phase-two scope adjustments or procurement-language revisions if material changes are identified.

Commission an external consulting engagement (months 1–2 and ongoing quarterly) with a leading digital-identity research organization (e.g., the EU's European Digital Identity Lab or a recognized security research firm) to conduct horizon scanning on mobile authentication, platform-integrity requirements, and fallback-path validation standards, publishing quarterly brief findings and flagging implications for the project's demonstrator scope and procurement language.

Integrate adaptive scope-review gates into the project timeline at months 8 (end of phase one) and month 16 (mid-phase three), explicitly authorizing the project team to revise demonstrator scope or procurement language if horizon-scanning analysis reveals that critical technical assumptions have been invalidated, with pre-authorized budget reallocation to accommodate scope changes without requiring additional government approval.
review: Controls strategic adaptability vs. project scope creep. Weakness: The options fail to consider that frequent scope adjustments, even if technically justified, may erode stakeholder confidence in project stability, or that horizon-scanning may identify genuine technical improvements that are too incremental to justify scope revision, consuming effort on analysis without actionable outcomes.
